questions.question: What is a behavioral experiment in CBT?

options.text: Trying new behaviors randomly to see what happens
Testing the accuracy of a specific thought or prediction through real-world action
Forcing yourself to do things that scare you without planning
Observing other people's behavior to learn social skills

questions.explanation: Correct. A behavioral experiment is structured hypothesis testing: 'I predict X will happen if I do Y. Let me test it and see what actually happens.' This generates evidence that either confirms or contradicts your thought, making beliefs testable rather than assumed.

questions.question: Why do you make a specific prediction before conducting a behavioral experiment?

options.text: To make the experiment more scientific and formal
To create a clear standard for evaluating whether the thought was accurate
To guarantee the experiment will succeed
To convince yourself the negative thought is definitely wrong

questions.explanation: Yes. If your thought is 'Everyone will judge me if I speak up,' you predict 'At least 3 people will visibly react negatively.' After the experiment, you compare prediction to reality. This prevents your brain from reinterpreting the outcome to match the original fear.

questions.question: What should you do if the outcome of a behavioral experiment confirms your negative prediction?

options.text: Give up — this proves the negative thought was right all along
Ignore the outcome and pretend it didn't happen
Examine what happened objectively: was it as catastrophic as feared? What can you learn?
Immediately repeat the experiment to prove it was a fluke

questions.explanation: Exactly. Sometimes the negative outcome does happen — but it's rarely as catastrophic as predicted. Ask: 'How bad was it really? How did I cope? What would I do differently next time?' Even 'negative' experiments teach you that you can handle discomfort and learn from setbacks.

questions.question: How do behavioral experiments differ from exposure therapy?

options.text: They are the same thing — just different names
Behavioral experiments test specific predictions; exposure focuses on habituation to feared stimuli
Behavioral experiments are easier and less anxiety-provoking
Exposure therapy doesn't work; behavioral experiments are the only effective approach

questions.explanation: Correct. Behavioral experiments ask 'What will happen if I do this?' and collect data on a specific thought. Exposure therapy builds tolerance to anxiety through repeated, graduated contact with feared situations. Both are valuable; they serve different purposes.

questions.question: Design a behavioral experiment for a thought you've been working with. What's the specific prediction? What will you do to test it? What outcome would contradict the negative thought? How will you measure the result?
